metadata.title: Research the Protein Corona: How the Body Immediately Overwrites Your Nanoparticle Design
metadata.color_semantics: teardown: INK #2A1A0E neutral/normal; CRIMSON #C8102E key finding/vulnerability; SLATE #545454 structure/neutral

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. The body overwrites your design in thirty seconds.

lines: The body overwrites your design in thirty seconds.

beats.narration_text: You spend months engineering a nanoparticle with targeting ligands on its surface — antibodies, peptides, folate — to direct it to cancer cells. You inject it into a patient. Within thirty seconds, a layer of serum proteins adsorbs onto the surface, completely burying your targeting ligands. The body has overwritten your design. What the immune system sees and what you designed are not the same thing.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — title card: PROTEIN CORONA: YOUR DESIGN MEETS BIOLOGY; thirty-second timeline: injection → protein adsorption → ligands buried → MPS clearance; the nanoparticle you injected is not the nanoparticle that reaches the tumor

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to research protein corona formation — adsorption kinetics, which proteins adsorb, the consequences for active targeting and clearance, and the strategies to minimize it.

props.command:
claude "Research protein corona formation on nanoparticles.
Explain: (1) kinetics — how fast hard and soft corona
form; (2) which proteins adsorb first (albumin,
apolipoproteins, fibrinogen, complement C3); (3)
consequences for active targeting (buried ligands),
immune clearance (opsonization → MPS uptake), and
pharmacokinetics; (4) strategies to minimize corona
(PEG coating, zwitterionic surfaces) and limitations;
(5) whether exploiting the corona is viable."

beats.role_note: terminal (Onda) — Python script reading Claude response
beats.narration_text: Read the code before trusting it. The script builds the corona timeline — what adsorbs first and when, using the Vroman effect: albumin arrives first then is displaced by higher-affinity proteins like apolipoproteins and complement. The consequence column shows what each protein recruits — opsonization, complement activation, MPS clearance.

props.code:
# protein_corona.py — Claude Code output
CORONA_TIMELINE = [
  {"time": "<30 s",  "layer": "soft",
   "proteins": ["albumin", "fibrinogen"],
   "consequence": "Vroman effect — albumin arrives first"},
  {"time": "<1 min", "layer": "hard",
   "proteins": ["apolipoprotein", "complement C3"],
   "consequence": "opsonization → MPS clearance"},
  {"time": ">1 min", "layer": "stable hard",
   "proteins": ["immunoglobulins", "vitronectin"],
   "consequence": "targeting ligands buried — new identity"},
]
print("PROTEIN CORONA TIMELINE (Vroman effect):")
for t in CORONA_TIMELINE:
    print(f"  {t['time']:8} {t['layer']:10} {t['consequence']}")

beats.role_note: OPEN vox slot → Manim video B04_ProteinCorona
beats.narration_text: Run it. The gold nanoparticle at the center carries crimson targeting ligands radiating outward. Then the soft corona arrives — albumin dots at the inner ring. Then the hard corona — a thick grey ring that buries the ligands entirely. The macrophage approaching from the left does not see your targeting ligands. It sees the complement proteins on the corona. That is what MPS clearance looks like at the molecular level.
beats.visual_intent: Manim B04_ProteinCorona: nanoparticle center → targeting ligands (CRIMSON lines) → soft corona (albumin dots) → hard corona (thick outer ring burying ligands) → macrophage approaching; annotation: THE CELL SEES THE CORONA, NOT YOUR LIGANDS

beats.narration_text: Fighting the corona is one strategy. Designing with it is another. Now iterate: ask Claude to compare pre-coated particles that adsorb a specific corona you choose versus eliminating the corona entirely with zwitterionic surfaces.

props.command:
claude "Compare two protein corona strategies: (a) designing
a pre-coated particle that adsorbs a specific corona
you choose (albumin-coated for stealth, apolipoprotein
A-I corona for liver targeting), vs. (b) eliminating
the corona entirely with zwitterionic surfaces. What
evidence exists for each in vivo? Has either entered
clinical trials?"

beats.narration_text: Pre-coating with albumin reduces but does not eliminate corona formation — and the albumin coat itself can be displaced by apolipoproteins in minutes. Apolipoprotein A-I pre-coating is a deliberate liver-targeting strategy: you recruit a corona that routes the nanoparticle to hepatocytes. Zwitterionic surfaces reduce protein adsorption by up to ninety percent in vitro, but in vivo the reduction is closer to fifty percent. No zwitterionic nanoparticle has cleared a phase two trial.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — two-strategy comparison: CORONA EXPLOITATION (albumin/apolipoprotein precoating → deliberate routing) vs CORONA ELIMINATION (zwitterionic, <50% reduction in vivo); clinical status: neither in phase 2 approval

beats.narration_text: So the protein corona is the body's first-pass editing of your design. The nanoparticle you engineer is not the nanoparticle that reaches the tumor — it is the corona-coated particle that results from your design interacting with the biological environment. The field spent a decade fighting this. Some groups are now designing with it.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — summary card: THE CORONA IS NOT YOUR ENEMY — IT IS THE PRODUCT; your design + biological environment = the actual nanoparticle; designing with corona vs fighting it

beats.narration_text: Your move: run your nanoparticle in fifty percent human serum and re-measure the size by DLS. If the hydrodynamic diameter increased by more than thirty nanometers, you have a hard corona. Then check the zeta potential — if it neutralized toward zero, complement proteins have adsorbed. Those two measurements tell you whether your active targeting is still functional.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — next steps card: 1) incubate NP in 50% human serum, re-measure DLS (>30 nm increase = hard corona); 2) check zeta potential (neutralized = complement adsorption); 3) verify targeting ligand accessibility post-corona by competitive binding assay

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
